repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem
Opening .tar.gz or .zip URLs shows raw binary instead of using the archive plugins.

## Solution
Following original implementation (`netrw`), the plugin needs to detect archive files URLs, download to a temp file and open them with tar/zip handlers. We keep existing behavior for other URLs.

## Out of Scope
Adding Lua equivalents of `zipPlugin.vim` and `tarPlugin.vim` that operate on buffers instead of file paths.

Closes #38725

comment_data.body: test is nearly identical to the one above
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -113,4 +113,54 @@ describe('vim.net.request', function()
     t.eq(true, content[1]:find('Here') ~= nil)
     t.eq(true, content[2]:find('html') ~= nil)
   end)
+
+  it('opens remote tar.gz URLs as tar archives', function()
+    t.skip(skip_integ, 'NVIM_TEST_INTEG not set: skipping network integration test')
+
+    local rv = exec_lua([[
+      vim.cmd('runtime! plugin/net.lua')
+      vim.cmd('runtime! plugin/tarPlugin.vim')
+
+      vim.cmd('edit https://github.com/neovim/neovim/releases/download/nightly/nvim-macos-x86_64.tar.gz')
+
+      vim.wait(2500, function()
+        return vim.bo.filetype == 'tar' or vim.b.tarfile ~= nil
+      end)
+
+      return {
+        filetype = vim.bo.filetype,
+        modified = vim.bo.modified,
+        tarfile = vim.b.tarfile ~= nil,
+      }
+    ]])
+
+    t.eq('tar', rv.filetype)
+    t.eq(false, rv.modified)
+    t.eq(true, rv.tarfile)
+  end)
+
+  it('opens remote zip URLs as zip archives', function()

comment_data.body: hmm, I guess `vim.fs.ext()` doesn't work here because it's a multi-suffix. I wonder if we should add a parameter to ext() @yochem
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -6,6 +6,32 @@ vim.g.loaded_nvim_net_plugin = true
 local augroup = vim.api.nvim_create_augroup('nvim.net.remotefile', {})
 local url_patterns = { 'http://*', 'https://*' }
 
+local archive_patterns = {
+  { suffix = '.tar.gz', kind = 'tar' },
+  { suffix = '.tar.bz2', kind = 'tar' },
+  { suffix = '.tar.xz', kind = 'tar' },
+  { suffix = '.txz', kind = 'tar' },
+  { suffix = '.tar', kind = 'tar' },
+  { suffix = '.zip', kind = 'zip' },
+}
+
+---@param url string
+---@return string
+local function url_path(url)
+  return (url:gsub('[?#].*$', ''))
+end
+
+---@param url string
+---@return 'zip'|'tar'?
+local function archive_type(url)
+  local path = url_path(url)
+  for _, pattern in ipairs(archive_patterns) do
+    if vim.endswith(path, pattern.suffix) then

comment_data.body:
```suggestion
      -- XXX: zipPlugin.vim, tarPlugin.vim don't work with non-file buffers.
      local tmpfile = vim.fn.tempname()
```

comment_data.body:
That was intentional. Even if the tests are almost identical they cover two different plugins with separate external dependencies.

`tarPlugin.vim `->`tar`
`zipPlugin.vim` -> `zip` / `unzip`

Happy to consolidate them in one test though

comment_data.body:
Yes `vim.archive` is a good candidate. I had in mind for the net plugin to just dispatch to a generic open method in `vim.archive` and let that module deal with the buffer lifeycle (validate if buffer is indeed an archive file and either open it or fallback). 

For the `vim.filetype`, maybe that could be approached as a separate behaviour i.e URL aware `vim.filetype` matching logic.

If we are good on `vim.archive` beign responsible for this we could let it own the logic for identifying archives (e.g checking headers per POSIX tar, zip signatures) rather than relying on filename-based detection. Then `vim.filetype` could depend on that for classification instead of matching just on filenames alone.

comment_regular_data.body:
Instead of the temp file approach, could we detect the filetype from the basename of the url and handle that accordingly?

Or is there a general way to "stack" `BufReadCmd`s?

> Instead of the temp file approach, could we detect the filetype from the basename of the url and handle that accordingly?
> 
> Or is there a general way to "stack" `BufReadCmd`s?

Ohh so just read the arhcive url -> write to buffer -> use zip/tar plugin from buffer?


Edit:
I checked the `tarPlugin.vim` and it expects a file on disk, which matches with the old netrw behavior: download file to a tmp file, hand it to `tar#Browse`/`zip#Browse`. 

Given that, the current fix with temp-file is reasonable. We get to restore previous behaviour with minimal changes and then later we can consider a more buffer oriented approach with a refactor on tar/zip plugins.

Edit 2:
You are right @yochem . I'm having second thoughts about calling this "restore previous behaviour", since we are effectly making now `:edit`  download a file instead of keeping everythign buffer first as originally introduced in #34140. 

Open to suggestions on a cleaner way to approach this or if we should do it in a seperate PR.
Wrote down a proposal on how we could rework the legacy plugins so this behaviour can be done as buffer first #38760 later

I will go ahead and merge but please consider adding a retry to these integ tests in `net_spec`. The failing CI in this PR happens to be such a failure :) Though ironically, it's one of the existing tests.

Also wondering if it will help avoid failures to fetch a smaller tar.gz / .zip file.

```
FAILED   test/functional/lua/net_spec.lua @ 60: vim.net.request calls on_response with error on 404 (async failure)
test/functional/lua/net_spec.lua:8: Expected HTTP 404 or exit code 22, got: curl: (35) Recv failure: Connection reset by peer


stack traceback:
	test/functional/lua/net_spec.lua:8: in function 'assert_404_error'
	test/functional/lua/net_spec.lua:75: in function <test/functional/lua/net_spec.lua:60>
```

title: fix(net): handle remote archive URLs via tar/zip browse
